Description: Communication devices
Explanation: Those with dysgraphia (Specific Learning Disorder for WRITING) struggle with the physical and cognitive aspects of writing, leading to messy or slow handwriting, poor spelling, difficulty organizing thoughts on paper, grammar issues, and trouble putting ideas into coherent sentences, despite often having strong verbal skills. Key struggles include letter formation, spacing, grammar, spelling, punctuation, and organizing ideas. The impacts of these struggles flow into many curriculum areas, and should not be viewed as a 'iteracy' or 'English' problem.

Description: Listening to texts - read aloud, e-books,
Explanation: Reading is a complex task, involving TECHNICAL SKILLS such as phonemic awareness (sounds), phonics (letter-sound connection) and fluency (smooth reading) as well as COMPREHENSION SKILLS  such as literal and inferential comprehension, broad vocabulary, making connections, understanding context and subtext and critical thinking, plus MONITORING SKILLS such as focus, attention and self-correction. Internal and external 'voice' add to comprehension and self-correction and give character, personality and tone to the reading experience.

Description: Meets curriculum standard with appropriate consistency
Explanation: Early Years, at Level is defined by curriculum

Description: Does not meet curriculum standard. Needs extensive support to meet curriculum standard
Explanation: Early Years, at Level is defined by curriculum

Description: Can 'read' (in whatever form is appropriate - see above) and understand the text in the context of being able to identify information within that text ("Show where it says....")
Explanation: Literal comprehension is about recall (remembering) and associating (connecting with something we already understand). It therefore relies on memory and recognition. It is understanding what the text explicitly (concretely, in black and white, on the page) says. Literal comprehension fits into levels 1 & 2 of the heirarchy of thinking skills (UNDERSTAND and REMEMBER) Often referred to as 'Reading WITHIN the lines'.

Description: No understanding or mis-understanding of text appropriate to the academic level. Requires extensive support to make literal meaning from text.
Explanation: Literal comprehension is about recall (remembering) and associating (connecting with something we already understand). It therefore relies on memory and recognition. It is understanding what the text explicitly (concretely, in black and white, on the page) says. Literal comprehension fits into levels 1 & 2 of the heirarchy of thinking skills (UNDERSTAND and REMEMBER) Often referred to as 'Reading WITHIN the lines'.

Description: Can use clues within the text to infer / realise / connect to / make understanding of and draw conclusions from the text. Has some understanding of figurative language. Has moved beyond literal ways of making meaning.
Explanation: Inferential comprehension is making connections and drawing conclusions based on clues and prior knowledge. It is relies on understanding context and subtext and brings into play literary features like sarcasm, similies, metaphors, idiom and analogy.  Inferential comprehension fits in level 3 of the heirarchy of thinking skills (APPLY) Often referred to as 'Reading BETWEEN the lines'.

Description: Has purely literal or no understanding of the text. Does not understand figurative language. Requires extensive support to 'join the dots'/make connections in order to make inferences.
Explanation: Inferential comprehension is making connections and drawing conclusions based on clues and prior knowledge. It is relies on understanding context and subtext and brings into play literary features like sarcasm, similies, metaphors, idiom and analogy.  Inferential comprehension fits in level 3 of the heirarchy of thinking skills (APPLY) Often referred to as 'Reading BETWEEN the lines'.

Description: Can combine evidence from the text with personal knowledge, further research and educated opinion to make judgements about that text. Can evalute structure, quality, relevance and content and justify this evaluation. Shows metalinguistic awareness (how language can be manipulated for effect)
Explanation: Analytical comprehension is making judgements about a text and connecting it to personal experience. It involves analysing the author's craft for structure, quality,  content, effectiveness, voice, tone. engagement and forming opinions based on evidence, personal experience, and external criteria. It evaluates how the author has written the text and why it matters. Analytical comprehension fits in levels 4 & 5 of the heirarchy of thinking skills (ANALYSE & EVALUATE) Often referred to as 'Reading BEYOND the lines'.

Description: Demonstrates purely literal or inferential comprehension. Cannot evaluate or justify the evaluation of a text beyond stating an opinion. Has no metalinguistic awareness.
Explanation: Analytical comprehension is making judgements about a text and connecting it to personal experience. It involves analysing the author's craft for structure, quality,  content, effectiveness, voice, tone. engagement and forming opinions based on evidence, personal experience, and external criteria. It evaluates how the author has written the text and why it matters. Analytical comprehension fits in levels 4 & 5 of the heirarchy of thinking skills (ANALYSE & EVALUATE) Often referred to as 'Reading BEYOND the lines'.

Description: Positive intention / attempts to read
Explanation: Monitoring skills include focus, attention and self-correction as well as the internal organisation of progress tracking (where am I up to and what has happened so far?)

Description: Refusal to use tools and strategies that support reading (underlying reasons for this need to be addressed)
Explanation: Monitoring skills include focus, attention and self-correction as well as the internal organisation of progress tracking (where am I up to and what has happened so far?)

Description: Some minor lapses in concentration followed by re-focus
Explanation: Monitoring crosses between skills-based and behavioural. It is crucial to distinguish between the two rather than assume that behaviour = skill.

Description: Prioritising socialising over focus (may require social/behavioural intervention)
Explanation: Monitoring crosses between skills-based and behavioural. It is crucial to distinguish between the two rather than assume that behaviour = skill.

Description: Re-reading sections of text (making meaning, clarifying, refocusing)
Explanation: What is deemed 'appropriate' in terms of focus and attention is often categorised by chronological age/ year level,  but in the case of disability should be looked at in terms of developmental age/stage

Description: Robotic/monotone reading or pretending to read when capable (question and address engagement/interest)
Explanation: What is deemed 'appropriate' in terms of focus and attention is often categorised by chronological age/ year level,  but in the case of disability should be looked at in terms of developmental age/stage

Description: Reading in ways that support focus for the individual (may include rocking, fidgeting, 'unusual' seating positions/lying down, using chair bands or under desk cycle machines.
Explanation: Focus and attention are hugely impacted by diagnosis such as ADHD and ASD where sensory and physiological factors alter the experience of being. Where one student may be more focused when still, another may need to move in order to pay attention and remain focused.

Description: Reading without comprehension (unless basic technical skills are the focus)
Explanation: Focus and attention are hugely impacted by diagnosis such as ADHD and ASD where sensory and physiological factors alter the experience of being. Where one student may be more focused when still, another may need to move in order to pay attention and remain focused.

Description: Needing to clarifying meaning / vocabulary
Explanation: It should be assumed that monitoring skills need explicit teaching. Some students will not require it but many will and may not have 'mastered' these skills in middle/senior years

Description: No interest in clarifying/making meaning (may be a social/confidence concern. Ensure a variety of ways to ask questions are available)
Explanation: It should be assumed that monitoring skills need explicit teaching. Some students will not require it but many will and may not have 'mastered' these skills in middle/senior years

Description: Dynamic progress (not always consistently improving but generally making progress)
Explanation: Concerns regarding ability to engage with different text types, genre, technical reading fall generally under the banner of 'comprehension'.

Description: Ongoing lack of progress
Explanation: Concerns regarding ability to engage with different text types, genre, technical reading fall generally under the banner of 'comprehension'.

Description: Improved focus and skills with texts of interest and/or that are familiar
Explanation: Concerns regarding ability to engage with different text types, genre, technical reading fall generally under the banner of 'comprehension'.

Description: Visual examples of content (pictures to support understanding)
Explanation: Manipulating numbers is a complex task, involving NUMBER SENSE (what numbers mean, their relationships to each other, and their magnitudes). MENTAL ARITHMETIC & COMPUTATIONAL FLUENCY ( the ability to perform calculations "in your head" quickly and accurately). MATHEMATICAL OPERATIONS AND TECHNIQUES (dealing with parts of numbers, algebraic thinking, order of operations) DATA ANALYSIS AND LOGICAL REASONING (including patterns and statistics) MEASUREMENT AND SPATICAL SKILLS (geometry, unit conversion) PRACTICAL APPLICATION ( financial literacy and use of number tools such as calculators and spreadsheets)

Description: Number chart, number line, place value chart / columns
Explanation: Manipulating numbers is a complex task, involving NUMBER SENSE (what numbers mean, their relationships to each other, and their magnitudes). MENTAL ARITHMETIC & COMPUTATIONAL FLUENCY ( the ability to perform calculations "in your head" quickly and accurately). MATHEMATICAL OPERATIONS AND TECHNIQUES (dealing with parts of numbers, algebraic thinking, order of operations) DATA ANALYSIS AND LOGICAL REASONING (including patterns and statistics) MEASUREMENT AND SPATICAL SKILLS (geometry, unit conversion) PRACTICAL APPLICATION ( financial literacy and use of number tools such as calculators and spreadsheets)

Description: Extra time
Explanation: For students who struggles with manipulating numbers, challenge may lie in one or more of these skill development areas AND/OR in their mental or physical organisation skills, AND/ OR in juggling the mental load required to combine task requirements.

Description: Grid books or grid charts to support formatting
Explanation: For students who struggles with manipulating numbers, challenge may lie in one or more of these skill development areas AND/OR in their mental or physical organisation skills, AND/ OR in juggling the mental load required to combine task requirements.

Description: Step-by-step explicit teaching
Explanation: It is extremely common for students to be succeeding at different levels for different aspects of dealing with numbers.

Description: Annotated, worked examples
Explanation: It is extremely common for students to be succeeding at different levels for different aspects of dealing with numbers.

Description: Visual examples of others working with and explaining numbers (what 'makes sense' to one student may not to the next - find what works)
Explanation: Some students need to learn by rote until the pattern of how to work a specific type of problem is 'cemented' in their brain, others need to understand the logic and conceptual basis of a skill. Neither is wrong and it is not helpful to force either type of learner to endure (or be confused by) sitting though a lesson that does not serve them. This may impact class or within class organisation.

Description: Hands-on manipulatives
Explanation: Some students need to learn by rote until the pattern of how to work a specific type of problem is 'cemented' in their brain, others need to understand the logic and conceptual basis of a skill. Neither is wrong and it is not helpful to force either type of learner to endure (or be confused by) sitting though a lesson that does not serve them. This may impact class or within class organisation.

Description: Gradual and logical / sequential build up of skills
Explanation: As a subject that is very logical, with clear 'right and wrong' answers, perfectionism and global 'all or nothing' thinking (either I'm good at Maths or I'm terrible) are common. Segmenting topics (teaching, tasks and marking) can serve to avoid or undermine this negative generalising.
